title: TBM 354: The 4 Framework Jobs (And Why It Matters)
questions: When adopting a framework, did we explicitly agree on what job we’re hiring it for?
How can a framework intended as a thinking tool get operationalized into a rigid process, and what happens then?
Do different people in our organization define “experience” differently, and how does that shape their view of frameworks as crutches or aids?
Is our current framework being used for teaching, local alignment, federated coordination, or quality assurance—and is that usage obvious and agreed upon?
summary: This post explains that product teams should treat frameworks as tools with distinct “jobs” rather than as universal processes. It’s for product leaders and practitioners who want faster alignment and coordination across experience levels and teams, and who have seen frameworks get misapplied or over-embedded into rigid workflows.

title: Software Development As Collective Learning With Hazel Weakly
questions: How do we shift from individual productivity or force-multiplier thinking to collective learning at scale?
What does “architecture” mean when it’s meant to enable coherent autonomy and emergent alignment?
How can teams run migrations safely without relying on sunken-cost momentum?
What conditions make knowledge work fruitful rather than just “productive”?
summary: This post discusses software development and leadership through the lens of collective learning, emergence, and socio-technical alignment, aimed at platform engineering and people leaders. It argues that “force multiplier” thinking is insufficient and that architecture should enable coherent autonomy so local decisions bubble up into global coherence. It also reframes migrations and retrospection as iterative, de-risked, and feedback-driven processes rather than disruptive one-shot change.

title: TBM 55/52: Making a Model (Step-by-Step)
questions: How do you go about making your models and frameworks step by step?
What inputs and outputs should you map when building a community health model?
How do you incorporate prior work without copying it blindly?
How can you turn messy sticky-note themes into a flywheel model that supports prioritization?
summary: This post is a practical, step-by-step walkthrough of how to build a model from scratch, using live diagramming and iterative refinement in a tool like Miro. It’s aimed at people (especially in product/growth and community work) who want a repeatable method for turning messy ideas into a structured framework that can support prioritization and discussion.

title: TBM 54/52: "That Framework Is Just Common Sense!"
questions: When should a product management framework be treated as a starting point rather than something to implement?
How do we ensure a framework teaches skills instead of becoming a job aid?
What role do tacit skills, practice, and feedback play in making frameworks effective?
How can teams mix multiple frameworks to improve product work?
search_terms: frameworks as job aids vs teaching
learning experience design for product teams
summary: The post argues that product management frameworks can feel like common sense, but their real value depends on how they’re used. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who want to avoid “implementing” frameworks as ends in themselves and instead use them to teach underlying skills through practice, feedback, and good instruction. The author recommends mixing and matching frameworks rather than relying on any single one.

title: TBM 46/52: (F)acts, (A)ssumptions, (B)eliefs
questions: How can our team quickly get aligned on the foundational premises behind our work?
Which of our assumptions are we operating on now versus testing for the future?
How do we track and update facts, assumptions, and beliefs over time without losing context?
What should we do when everything seems to change all the time?
summary: The post proposes a lightweight team exercise—facts, assumptions, beliefs (FAB)—to improve alignment on the underlying premises driving day-to-day work. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who want a practical way to distinguish operating assumptions from hypotheses to test, assign confidence levels, and create a recurring review ritual with historical context. It also extends the idea to both company-wide and team-level alignment (fractal FAB).

title: TBM 39B/52: Crawl, Walk, Run, and the Fragility of Frameworks
questions: When should a company rely on a framework versus creating the right learning conditions?
How do “crawl, walk, run” narratives distort responsibility and accountability in teams?
What conditions (psychological safety, tooling, feedback loops) are necessary for people to learn effectively?
How can leaders avoid blaming teams for slow progress when frameworks are a poor fit?
search_terms: how teams learn in healthy environments
summary: The post argues that “crawl, walk, run” style process frameworks often function as synthetic control tools for less-than-ideal organizations, shifting blame to teams when adoption or outcomes lag. It’s aimed at leaders, consultants, and coaches who want to improve capability by building the real conditions for learning—psychological safety, coaching, appropriate challenges, and tight feedback—rather than treating framework compliance as the path to performance.

title: TBM 25/52: In Defense of Frameworks (and Process)
questions: How can we tell whether a framework is a useful tool or an unsafe goal-conformance mechanism?
What makes a framework context-aware and safe to apply beyond its original setting?
How do we surface and audit the implicit models and biases embedded in our leadership and performance systems?
What practices help teams co-design processes that newcomers can adopt and that expire when outdated?
summary: This post argues that frameworks and processes are unavoidable because humans think in patterns, and they can reduce cognitive load when used well. It is aimed at managers and teams who want to adopt process without falling into context-free, goal-conformance, or bias-driven misuse, and who should instead treat frameworks as tools with clear intent, context, and expiration.

title: Do We Need More Product Frameworks?
questions: Do we need more product frameworks, or better ways to use existing ones?
How can teams avoid confusing framework adoption with building real skill?
What context and history should we investigate before adopting a framework?
When is reinventing a framework beneficial versus harmful?
summary: The post argues that new product frameworks are usually repackaged versions of older ideas, and that this is generally fine when done intentionally. It’s aimed at product teams and framework writers who want to adopt or create frameworks without falling into the trap of treating the framework as the goal rather than understanding the underlying problem, context, and limits.
